one_line_takeaway: A large-scale (>50K models) apples-to-apples benchmark showing that with proper hyperparameter tuning, Masked-Small-Gradients and Convolution-Transpose are the most reliable, fast, and MIA/U-LiRA-resistant approximate unlearning methods -- and that the field's standard FT/GA/SRL baselines are inadequate.
key_finding: MSG ranks 1st in both Retention Deviation and Indiscernibility aggregated across datasets/architectures; CT ranks 1st in Indiscernibility and 3rd in Retention Deviation; both resist the stronger U-LiRA attack, unlike most methods. FT is a strong utility baseline (best aggregated RetDev) but poor on privacy (only 3rd on Indiscernibility) because it never explicitly perturbs forget-set-relevant weights. GA is consistently worst; its variant NG+ is a far better baseline (ranks ~5th on both). Efficiency: CT is fastest (up to 17.5x faster than retraining on CIFAR-10, avg 10.59x on ResNet18); MSG averages 5.3x. A striking reliability finding: even the perfectly Retrained model has ~10% of CIFAR-100 forget-set images flagged as 'forgotten members' by U-MIA across all 10 seeds, and 59.6%/65.2% of forget images are flagged for at least 5 seeds -- so absolute unlearning is unachievable/unmeasurable and rankings must be averaged across seeds. The paper concludes that evaluating a new MU method only against FT/GA/SRL is inadequate.
core_contribution: An empirical benchmark ('Deep Unlearn') of 18 state-of-the-art approximate machine-unlearning (MU) methods for image classification, evaluated across 5 datasets, 2 DNN architectures (ResNet18 and an ~11M-parameter TinyViT), and 10 shared initialization seeds. The paper's own contribution is the standardized, hyperparameter-tuned evaluation pipeline and the resulting rankings, NOT the MU methods themselves (all 18 are drawn from prior work / the NeurIPS'2023 Kaggle competition). The authors argue: (Q1) commonly used baselines are inadequate -- Gradient Ascent (GA) is consistently poor and should be replaced by Negative Gradient Plus (NG+), while Fine-tuning (FT) is a reliable baseline; (Q2/Q3) Masked-Small-Gradients (MSG) and Convolution-Transpose (CT) are consistently the most reliable and accurate across datasets, architectures, and seeds, and uniquely resist a strong per-sample attack (U-LiRA).
threat_model: The privacy threat is a membership inference adversary trying to determine whether specific 'forget-set' data points still influence a model after an unlearning method claims to have removed them. Two attack strengths are used as the evaluation adversary: (1) a population-based Unlearning-MIA (U-MIA), a logistic-regression classifier trained on losses over forget vs. test/validation points; and (2) a stronger per-sample Unlearning Likelihood Ratio Attack (U-LiRA) using shadow models. The motivating context is the 'right to be forgotten' (GDPR-style data deletion), removal of erroneous/biased training data, and post-hoc unlearning from already-deployed models where the training process cannot be modified. A successful unlearn drives the attack's accuracy toward random guessing (0.5).
limitations: Scope is image classification only (no LLMs, diffusion, or multimodal). A fixed uniform 10% forget budget -- no class-wise unlearning, no varying budgets, no iterative/sequential deletion. Several methods failed to produce usable models on hard settings (e.g., Fisher Forgetting FF failed on all 9 combinations; EU-k, IU, GA frequently in the Failed group), so comparisons are uneven. U-LiRA (the strongest attack) is only run on CIFAR-10/ResNet18 for the top performers due to cost, not across the whole grid. Only one ViT variant (TinyViT ~11M params) and one CNN (ResNet18); no large models. Rankings depend on the clustering cutoffs and the chosen loss weights (alpha=beta=gamma=1/3, eta=1), which are heuristic. The privacy metric itself is acknowledged to be a weak proxy: the authors note MIA/Indiscernibility can be gamed, which is precisely why U-LiRA was added, but the residual-membership floor (~10% even for retrained models) means the metrics cannot certify true removal.

quantitative_results: 18 MU methods x 9 dataset-architecture combos x 10 seeds; ~50,220 unlearned models in the main study (paper frames total effort as MU over 100K models)
MSG aggregated rank: 1st in Retention Deviation (G1 count 8/9) and 1st in Indiscernibility (G1 count 9/9)
CT aggregated rank: 3rd in Retention Deviation, 1st in Indiscernibility (G1 9/9)
FT aggregated rank: best in Retention Deviation (G1 8/9) but only 3rd in Indiscernibility

CIFAR-100 / ResNet18 (Table 4): Original model test-vs-forget MIA = 73% accuracy; most unlearned models ~50% (random). CT RetDev 0.07, Indisc 0.99, RTE 11.82. FT RetDev 0.05, Indisc 0.99, RTE 5.16. MSG RetDev 0.71, Indisc 1.00, RTE 4.49. GA RetDev 1.61, Indisc 0.90, RTE 39.97. Retrained (gold) RetDev 0.00, Indisc 0.99.
Run-time efficiency (ResNet18, Table 6): CT fastest, up to 17.5x faster than retraining on CIFAR-10, avg 10.59x; MSG avg 5.29x (7.57x on UTKFace, 3.32x on FashionMNIST)

Consistent Unforgettability (CIFAR-100): even the Retrained model has ~10% of forget images flagged across all 10 seeds; 59.6% and 65.2% of forget images flagged for >=5 seeds; some forget images correctly identified across all 10 seeds by 13 of 18 methods
U-LiRA: 640 shadow-based models (n=64) for the best performers on CIFAR-10/ResNet18; MSG and CT show resilience while others do not
Hyperparameter search: 3 sweeps x 100 trials = 300 configs per method (200 trials/sweep for U-LiRA); 10% uniform forget budget; fixed split seed s=123
baselines_compared: Fine-tuning (FT) -- classical baseline, found reliable for utility
Gradient Ascent (GA) -- classical baseline, found consistently poor
Negative Gradient Plus (NG+) -- proposed as a better replacement baseline for GA
Retrained-from-scratch model (gold-standard reference for RR/FR/TR, RetDev, RTE)
Competition methods: FCS, MSG, CFW, PRMQ, CT, KDE, RNI (top-7 NeurIPS'2023 Kaggle)
Literature methods: SalUN, CF-k, EU-k, SCRUB, BT, FF, IU
